neighborhoods.name: Chinatown
primary_groups: Chinese immigrants, primarily from Guangdong Province (Taishan/Toisan district)
neighborhoods.character: The oldest Chinatown in North America and the densest Chinese community outside Asia. Bounded by Bush, Kearny, Broadway, and Powell Streets. Anti-Chinese violence and legal restrictions confined residents to a few square blocks. The community was destroyed in the 1906 earthquake and fire and rebuilt in its current location. The bachelor society — created by immigration laws that excluded Chinese women — defined the community's social structure for decades.
key_institutions: Chinese Six Companies (Chinese Consolidated Benevolent Association)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} came from Guangdong Province, San Francisco's Chinatown was almost certainly the first American address — a few dense blocks where Toishanese was the lingua franca and the Six Companies served as government, court, and employment bureau for a community that white America had decided to exclude.

neighborhoods.name: North Beach / Little Italy
primary_groups: Italian immigrants from Liguria, Sicily, and other regions
neighborhoods.character: San Francisco's Italian quarter, centered on Columbus Avenue and Washington Square. Genoese and Ligurian fishermen dominated the community in the early decades, followed by Sicilians and Southern Italians. The neighborhood was the base for the Italian fishing fleet that worked the Bay and the sardine boats that sailed to Monterey. Amadeo Giannini founded the Bank of Italy (later Bank of America) here in 1904.
key_institutions: Bank of Italy / Bank of America (founded in North Beach)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If the {surname} family was Italian and came to San Francisco, North Beach was home — Washington Square was the piazza, the fishing fleet at the Wharf was the livelihood, and Saints Peter and Paul Church recorded every baptism and wedding.

neighborhoods.name: Western Addition / Fillmore District
primary_groups: African Americans from Texas, Louisiana, Arkansas, Oklahoma
neighborhoods.character: San Francisco's primary Black neighborhood, centered on Fillmore Street. The population exploded during WWII when Black workers came for shipyard and defense jobs. The Fillmore became a jazz and blues center — 'the Harlem of the West.' Urban renewal in the 1960s-70s demolished much of the neighborhood, displacing thousands of Black families.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} came to San Francisco for war work in the {decade}s, the Fillmore was the neighborhood — the jazz clubs on Fillmore Street were open all night, the shipyard paychecks were fat, and for the first time a Black family from {origin_state} could walk the streets of a city without Jim Crow signs.

neighborhoods.name: South of Market (SoMa) / South of the Slot
neighborhoods.character: South of Market Street — 'South of the Slot,' named for the cable car tracks on Market — was San Francisco's working-class district. Boarding houses, rooming houses, and residential hotels housed single men who worked on the waterfront, in warehouses, and in light industry. The Irish dominated the neighborhood in the 19th century. Filipino workers settled in the adjacent Manilatown district along Kearny Street.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: South of Market was where working men without families lived — if {ancestor_name} arrived in San Francisco alone, a rooming house South of the Slot was probably the first address, with the waterfront hiring hall a short walk away.

major_employers.name: Waterfront / Longshoremen
major_employers.wages: Variable before unionization. After the 1934 General Strike and ILWU contracts: $1.10/hr (1934), rising to $3+/hr (1960s) with hiring hall protections that eliminated the shape-up.
major_employers.working_conditions: San Francisco's waterfront was the site of the 1934 General Strike — the defining event in West Coast labor history. Longshoremen Harry Bridges led the ILWU, which transformed waterfront work from casual exploitation into unionized employment with a hiring hall, guaranteed hours, and pensions. Before unionization, the shape-up system and employer blacklists made the work precarious.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The waterfront made San Francisco a union town — if {ancestor_name} worked the docks, the 1934 strike was the dividing line between the old days of the shape-up and the new era of the ILWU hiring hall.

major_employers.name: Southern Pacific Railroad
major_employers.wages: Railroad wages varied by skill and race. Section hands: $1.25-2.00/day. Skilled mechanics: $3-5/day. Railroad jobs offered relative stability.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Southern Pacific was California's dominant railroad and one of San Francisco's largest employers. Chinese workers built the transcontinental railroad but were excluded from most railroad jobs after completion. Irish, Italian, and Mexican workers filled subsequent positions. The railroad's political and economic power shaped California for decades.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The Southern Pacific Railroad ran California like a private kingdom — if {ancestor_name} worked for the railroad, the paycheck was steady but the company's power over its workers and over the state itself was absolute.

major_employers.name: Shipbuilding (Bethlehem Steel / Hunters Point Naval Shipyard)
major_employers.wages: Defense wages: $1.00-1.50/hr (1942-1945). Steady wartime employment with overtime.
major_employers.working_conditions: WWII shipbuilding transformed San Francisco's workforce. The Hunters Point Naval Shipyard and Bethlehem Steel's yard at Potrero Point employed tens of thousands. The wartime labor shortage opened these jobs to Black workers and women for the first time. Kaiser Shipyards across the Bay in Richmond employed over 90,000 workers.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} came to San Francisco during the war, the shipyards were probably the reason — the government needed ships faster than anyone thought possible, and for the first time, they were hiring regardless of race.

port_of_entry_details.facility: Angel Island Immigration Station (1910-1940) in San Francisco Bay processed Pacific immigrants, primarily Chinese and Japanese arrivals. Earlier arrivals entered through the Pacific Mail Steamship Company docks at the foot of First Street. The station was notorious for lengthy detentions of Chinese immigrants under the Chinese Exclusion Act.
port_of_entry_details.processing: Chinese immigrants faced interrogation that could last weeks or months. Inspectors asked detailed questions about village geography and family relationships, designed to trap fraudulent 'paper sons.' Japanese immigrants faced less hostile but still rigorous processing. Angel Island records are now at the National Archives Pacific Region (San Bruno).
records_generated: Angel Island immigration case files (National Archives, San Bruno)
Great Register of Voters (records nationality and naturalization)

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Boarding houses, residential hotels, and rooming houses for single men. Chinese workers lived in dormitory-style housing in Chinatown. Irish and Italian families rented flats in frame Victorian houses. The 1906 earthquake and fire destroyed most of the city's housing stock, forcing wholesale rebuilding.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Chinese confined to Chinatown by law and violence. Italians in North Beach. Irish in the Mission District and SoMa. Japanese in the Western Addition (Japantown). Black residents in the Fillmore (especially after WWII). Filipino workers in Manilatown. Each community's boundaries were enforced by social pressure, economic access, and in the case of Chinese residents, explicit legal restriction.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Rooming house or boarding house (months to years for single men) to rental flat in a Victorian (1-5 years) to homeownership (slow, due to San Francisco's high real estate costs). The city's housing stock of multi-unit Victorians meant that many families remained renters longer than in cities with cheaper housing.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: San Francisco was the epicenter of anti-Chinese sentiment in the United States. The Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882 was largely a product of California politics. Anti-Japanese agitation followed in the early 1900s. Each immigrant group faced organized hostility.
specific_incidents: 1877 anti-Chinese riots — Denis Kearney's Workingmen's Party rallies
1882 Chinese Exclusion Act — first federal law barring immigration by nationality
1906 school segregation of Japanese students, triggering international diplomatic crisis
1942 Japanese American internment from the Bay Area
nativist_organizations: Workingmen's Party of California (Denis Kearney, anti-Chinese, 1870s-1880s)
Native Sons of the Golden West (anti-Asian advocacy)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Chinese Consolidated Benevolent Association (Chinese Six Companies)
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Chinese immigrants organized by district of origin in Guangdong
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: District association membership records, dispute resolution records. Records are held by the individual associations and access varies. Some records at the Chinese Historical Society of America.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Six Companies governed Chinatown — if {ancestor_name} was Chinese, their district association (based on their home village or county in Guangdong) probably recorded their arrival, employment, and community standing.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Saints Peter and Paul Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death registers in Italian and English. Records held by the Archdiocese of San Francisco.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Saints Peter and Paul — the 'Italian Cathedral' on Washington Square — recorded the sacramental life of North Beach. If the {surname} family was part of San Francisco's Italian community, the parish registers likely hold their family records.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Third Baptist Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community in the Fillmore/Western Addition
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism records. One of the oldest Black churches west of the Mississippi.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Third Baptist Church is one of the oldest Black congregations west of the Rockies — if {ancestor_name} was part of San Francisco's wartime Black community, the church's records may document their arrival and integration into the Fillmore neighborhood.

source_references.author: Nayan Shah
source_references.title: Contagious Divides: Epidemics and Race in San Francisco's Chinatown
source_references.notes: Documents the intersection of public health policy and anti-Chinese racism in San Francisco, using medical records, census data, and municipal archives.

source_references.author: Albert S. Broussard
source_references.title: Black San Francisco: The Struggle for Racial Equality in the West, 1900-1954
source_references.notes: Comprehensive history of San Francisco's Black community covering the pre-WWII era, wartime migration, the Fillmore, and the struggle against discrimination.

source_references.author: Deanna Paoli Gumina
source_references.title: The Italians of San Francisco, 1850-1930
source_references.notes: Study of Italian immigrant settlement in North Beach, covering the fishing fleet, banking (Bank of Italy/Bank of America), parish life, and community institutions.

source_references.author: Erika Lee and Judy Yung
source_references.title: Angel Island: Immigrant Gateway to America
source_references.notes: Comprehensive history of Angel Island Immigration Station using detention records, oral histories, and poetry carved into the barracks walls by detainees.
